comment.body:
the goal is to keep uncertainity high.
increased IV and VIX makes the market very sensitive, even tho the variance itself if relatively low. this avoids a lot of strats to take ANY positions, while pushing tech lower through leveraged derivatives.
but it is clearly a trending market. models show it, with random momentums , very tiny-bit skewed towards mean reverting intraday, but really no significant. this is why i wrote in the post what i wrote.
i hold the puts, and look for intraday reversions from key lvls. spotting one move out, trade it then get lost.
AM and late PM. not chasing the moves. 

i showed yesterday, 2/2 intraday trade, highly green. 
and thats it.
market is clearly going down  + the infos i shared.

comment.body: Btw, that’s not a Nash Equilibrium. In the scene, a group of guys are checking out a group of girls, all of them preferring the blonde. Nash explains that if they all go for her, they’ll just get in each other’s way, and none of them will succeed. Going after her friends as a backup plan won’t work either, since they’ll be pissed about being the second choice. So Nash suggests they skip the blonde altogether and go straight for the friends, ensuring everyone pairs up while the blonde gets left out.

comment.body:
But here’s the problem, this isn’t actually a Nash Equilibrium. In a true Nash setup, no player has an incentive to switch strategies once they see what everyone else is doing. In this scene, though, every guy has a reason to break the plan and go for the blonde if they think the others won’t. That’s true for all of them.
A Nash Equilibrium only happens when no one wants to change their decision after seeing the outcome. But here, everyone has a reason to deviate. So yeah, cool scene, but not an equilibrium—just a stylized take on a coordination problem

comment.body:
The $VIX measures 30-day implied volatility for $SPX. Quick rule of thumb: divide the $VIX by 16 to estimate the expected daily move.
For example, VIX 16 = ±1% daily moves.
Right now with VIX at 22, the market's pricing in about ±1.4% per day.
But over the past couple weeks? We've been averaging just 1%. Feels tough to see $VIX pushing much higher unless things really pick up.
